items.title: Use minimal, trusted base images
items.description: Bloated base images (full OS distros) drastically expand the attack surface with shells, package managers, and unneeded binaries that aid post-exploitation. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Inspect the Dockerfile FROM lines and resulting image. Prefer distroless, scratch, or slim/alpine images from trusted sources. Confirm no compilers, package managers, or interactive shells remain in the production image. Common tags: image, hardening, attack-surface.

items.title: No secrets baked into image layers
items.description: Secrets copied or referenced during a build persist in intermediate layers even if deleted in a later layer, and can be extracted from the image history. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Run image history/layer analysis and filesystem extraction looking for API keys, private keys, .env files, cloud credentials, and tokens. Check ARG/ENV usage and COPY of credential files. Common tags: image, secrets, leakage.

items.title: Pin images to immutable digests, not mutable tags
items.description: Using mutable tags like :latest means the running image can silently change, breaking reproducibility and enabling tag-overwrite supply-chain attacks. Look for any use of :latest in production workloads. Common tags: image, supply-chain, pinning.

sections.title: Container Runtime Security — Privileged & Host Exposure

sections.title: Cluster Hardening & Supply Chain — CIS Benchmark & Control-Plane Hardening

items.title: Run kube-bench against control plane and nodes
items.description: The CIS Kubernetes Benchmark codifies hundreds of hardening checks; running kube-bench surfaces insecure flags and file permissions that manual review misses. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Execute kube-bench on control-plane and worker nodes, triage FAIL/WARN findings against the current CIS version, and confirm a remediation backlog with owners exists. Common tags: cis, benchmark, hardening.

items.title: Harden API server flags
items.description: Insecure API server flags (e.g. enabled insecure port, permissive authorization-mode, missing admission plugins) directly weaken cluster authentication and authorization. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Review kube-apiserver flags: --authorization-mode includes RBAC/Node, --insecure-port=0, profiling disabled, and required admission plugins (e.g. NodeRestriction) enabled. Common tags: cis, control-plane, hardening.

items.title: Harden kubelet configuration
items.description: Kubelet defaults can permit anonymous access, read-only port exposure, and unrotated certs, providing node and pod control to attackers. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Confirm kubelet has anonymous-auth disabled, authorization-mode Webhook, read-only-port 0, rotateCertificates enabled, and a tight protectKernelDefaults/streamingConnectionIdleTimeout posture. Common tags: cis, kubelet, hardening.

items.title: Harden the node OS and component file permissions
items.description: Insecure permissions on kubeconfig, manifests, and PKI files, plus an unhardened host OS, let a node-local attacker read credentials and tamper with components. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Confirm control-plane manifest/PKI files have restrictive ownership/modes (per CIS), the host OS is patched and CIS-hardened, and runtime threat detection (e.g. Falco) is deployed. Common tags: cis, node, hardening.
